coredata.dc:title: Second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal graphene quantum dots: Effects of size and structural defects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jiasheng
Li, Haipeng
Yang, Ziqiang
Zhang, Zitao
Shen, Xiaopeng
Han, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanosized fragments of graphene containing a bandgap caused by edge effects and quantum confinement. These features enable GQDs to have attractive photonics applications. During the preparation of graphene nanostructures, defects are unavoidable as they influence the behavior of π-electron transfer, which in turn impact the nonlinear optical (NLO) response. Herein, density functional theory was used to determine the electronic and three-order NLO properties of hexagonal GQDs with structural defects. The size of GQDs and the edge effects on their NLO properties were also investigated. Results showed with increased molecule size, the second hyperpolarizability increased for zigzag- and armchair-edged GQDs. This finding was due to the decrease in their frontier molecular-orbital energy gaps. Moreover, the introduction of point vacancy limited the π-electron transport in the zigzag-edged GQD, thereby widening the frontier molecular-orbital energy gap compared with the perfect zigzag-edged GQDs. However, in the presence of zigzag-edged GQDs having Stone–Wales defects, the energy gap decreased; consequently, the NLO response improved. All these findings provided new insights into the design of novel graphene-based NLO materials that may help expand the applications of GQDs in optoelectronic nanodevices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Nonlinear optics
Structural defect
Density functional theory

We are grateful to the Advanced Analysis and Computation Center of CUMT for the award of CPU hours to accomplish this work.

We are grateful to the Advanced Analysis and Computation Center of CUMT for the award of CPU hours to accomplish this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structures of different sized GQDs with hexagonal symmetry. Two typical patterns of hexagonal GQDs with armchair and zigzag edges are denoted as A-GQDs and Z-GQDs, respectively. Carbon atoms at the edges are saturated with hydrogen atoms, which are omitted for clarity. The numbers of carbon atoms is 18 (24), 42 (54), and 84 (96) for systems 1 (1′), 2 (2′), and 3 (3′), respectively.
(a) Frontier molecular-orbital energy and (b) their gap ΔEHL for armchair-edged GQDs (zigzag-edged GQDs). The x-axis shows the carbon atom number of GQDs: 1 (1′), 2 (2′), and 3 (3′).
Size effect of the second hyperpolarizability of studied GQDs.

Energy density of state (DOS) and molecular orbitals (MOs) of zigzag-edged GQDs. In the DOS maps, each discrete vertical line near the abscissa axis corresponds to an MO, and vertical dashed lines highlight the positions of HOMO (H) and LUMO (L): (a) pristine, (b) Stone–Wales defect, and (c) vacancy.

head.ce:title: Second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal graphene quantum dots: Effects of size and structural defects

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Physics
China University of Mining and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanosized fragments of graphene containing a bandgap caused by edge effects and quantum confinement. These features enable GQDs to have attractive photonics applications. During the preparation of graphene nanostructures, defects are unavoidable as they influence the behavior of π-electron transfer, which in turn impact the nonlinear optical (NLO) response. Herein, density functional theory was used to determine the electronic and three-order NLO properties of hexagonal GQDs with structural defects. The size of GQDs and the edge effects on their NLO properties were also investigated. Results showed with increased molecule size, the second hyperpolarizability increased for zigzag- and armchair-edged GQDs. This finding was due to the decrease in their frontier molecular-orbital energy gaps. Moreover, the introduction of point vacancy limited the π-electron transport in the zigzag-edged GQD, thereby widening the frontier molecular-orbital energy gap compared with the perfect zigzag-edged GQDs. However, in the presence of zigzag-edged GQDs having Stone–Wales defects, the energy gap decreased; consequently, the NLO response improved. All these findings provided new insights into the design of novel graphene-based NLO materials that may help expand the applications of GQDs in optoelectronic nanodevices.

ce:section.ce:para: Novel materials characterized by large nonlinear optical (NLO) properties have become a research hotspot considering their promising applications in photonic devices [1–5]. Organic π-conjugated materials are excellent NLO materials with a special electronic structure that endows them with short response time and large NLO response [6]. The delocalized π electrons in these materials and the charge transfer between electron donor and acceptor are reportedly responsible for their molecular hyperpolarizability [7–9]. However, the practical applications of organic NLO small molecules are limited because they are thermodynamically unstable under the conditions of high-power lasers. Additionally, in preparing all-optical switching, modulating, and computing devices through modern optical technology, the high third-order NLO response is crucial [10].

Recently, researchers have focused on NLO properties based on conjugated nanomaterials [11–13]. As a single layer of graphite sheet, pristine graphene is the basic unit of graphite, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, and other carbon materials [14]. In the graphene layer, the atoms are connected together by the sp2 σ bond, and a large area of conjugated π bond is formed by free electrons. This honeycomb-like structure endows graphene with high thermal conductivity [16], as well as outstanding electronic and optical properties [15]. However, graphene also has common disadvantages. For example, the gapless character of monolayer graphene may limit its applications in photonic devices [17]. The planar structure of graphene also leads to stacking and aggregation in solvents, leading to difficult dissolution in water and other organic solvents [18]. A recent study [19] has reported the successful chemical synthesis of small-size nanographenes, or graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which can be considered as the ‘building blocks’ of graphene. Considering the disruption of the infinite π-electron system of graphene in nanographene, its electronic and optical properties can become tunable by controlling the size, shape, and edges of the graphene fragment [20]. Zheng et al. [21] experimentally measured the third-order NLO response of GQDs by using nanosecond and picosecond Z-scan techniques and subsequently proposed nanographenes as potential NLO materials [22].

During nanographene growth or processing, defect formation is inevitable. Common structural defects include vacancies and atomic rearrangements, such as the Stone–Wales defect (a pentagon–heptagon pair) and other topology dislocations [23–25]. Physical properties can be modified by the structural defects and manipulations of artificial dopants [26,27]. Zhang et al. [28] synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GQDs and then experimentally investigated the doping effect on their optical properties. Ney et al. [29] studied graphene nanoflakes and found irradiation-enhanced paramagnetism upon the introduction of vacancies by irradiation with high-energy nitrogen ion beams. Hu et al. [30] theoretically studied zigzag graphene nanoribbons having different Stone–Wales defects and focused on its electronic structures and optical properties. Nakano et al. [31] calculated the third-order NLO properties of graphene nanoflakes characterized by diradicals with strong structural dependence. Otero et al. [32] reported a theoretical work on borazino (B3N3)-doped nanographene and its quadratic NLO properties. Although many studies have shown the significance of structural defects on electronic properties in nanomaterials [33,34], few have focused on the NLO properties of nanographene with structural defects. In such systems, the π-electron conjugation is able to propagate the density away from the defect center, indicating that the system size and edge effect require consideration [35]. In the present work, we proposed the breakage of the infinite plane hexagonal structure by introducing various defects and edges to tune the NLO properties of graphene. We used methods of density functional theory to systematically investigate the effect of structural defects including vacancy and Stone–Wales defect ring on the electronic properties and third-order NLO response of the finite-sized hexagonal GQDs compared with perfect GQDs. Structural defect is expected to be a new factor that can affect the tuning of hyperpolarizability. In addition, the size of the model system were also examined. It is true that spin effect has key role in the electronic properties of nanographene [36]. For instance, some special shaped NH2–GQD–NO2 systems with a singlet diradical or multiradical character exhibit very high second-order nonlinear optical response [25]; Muhammad et al. [27] found that the significant interplay between the diradical character and third-order nonlinear optical properties in fullerene systems. In the present work, we calculated the geometry and electronic properties of GQDs with close-shell singlet ground states. The spin effect, which was reported previously [31], is beyond the research of this work, because our study is focus on the effects of size and structural defects of second hyperpolarizability. This work could provide useful information for the development of graphene-based NLO materials.

In the field of nonlinear optics, molecule polarization using an external radiation field is commonly deemed as the creation of an induced dipole moment through an external electric field. A molecule's energy U perturbed by a static and uniform electric field F can be expanded as follows:
(1)
U
( F )
= U
( 0 )
−
∑ i
μ i
F i
−
1
2 !
∑
i j
α
i j
F i
F j
−
1
3 !
∑
i j k
β
i j k
F i
F j
F k
−
1
4 !
∑
i j k l
γ
i j k l
F i
F j
F k
F l
− . . .
where
U ( 0 )
is the total energy without the electric field;
μ i
is a component of the dipole-moment vector;
F i
is an electric-field component along the ith direction;
α
i j
is the electronic linear-polarizability tensor;
β
i j k
and
γ
i j k l
are the electronic first- and second-hyperpolarizability tensors, from which the macroscopic second- and third-order NLO susceptibilities originate, respectively; and i, j, k, and l correspond to the x, y, and z components, respectively.

The relationship between molecular structure and NLO properties could be understood by theoretically predicting hyperpolarizability. In the current work, the electronic second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal GQDs was calculated to determine the effect of structural defect on the third-order NLO response. The electronic contribution of the second hyperpolarizability γ is defined as follows [3]:
(2)
< γ > =
[
γ
x x x x
+
γ
y y y y
+
γ
z z z z
+ 2
(
γ
x x y y
+
γ
x x z z
+
γ
y y z z
)
]
/ 5

We fully optimized the close-shell ground-state geometrical structures of hexagonal GQDs with zigzag and armchair edges (Fig. 1 ) by using the hybrid Becke 3–Lee–Yang–Parr (B3LYP) [37] exchange-correlation functional with the 6–31G (d,p) basis set. Vibrational-frequency analysis was performed to examine all optimized geometries, and results showed no imaginary frequencies, indicating that the stable conformations were the equilibrium geometries. Under the restriction of hexagonal symmetry, the optimized geometries of GQD systems are planar without ripple formations. Through the analytical-derivative technique [38,39] based on density functional theory calculations, the second-hyperpolarizability tensor γ elements was obtained. CAM-B3LYP, the Coulomb attenuated hybrid exchange-correlation functional, was then proposed and successfully used to calculate (hyper)polarizabilities for charge-transfer systems [40,41]. Accordingly, we calculated the static second hyperpolarizability of gas-phase GQDs through the functional CAM-B3LYP. The adopted basis set of 6–31+G(d,p) included diffuse functions for high-accuracy hyperpolarizability evaluation. Additionally, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were calculated at the CAM-B3LYP/6-31+G(d,p) theory level. Gaussian 09 program package was used for all computations [42], whereas Multiwfn software [43] was used for wavefunction analysis.

The calculated HOMO energy, LUMO energy, and HOMO–LUMO energy gaps of different-sized hexagonal GQDs are shown in Fig. 2
. With increased size of armchair-edged GQDs, the HOMO energy level increased and the LUMO energy level decreased, leading to decreased HOMO–LUMO gap. These findings were similar to those for zigzag-edged GQDs. Variations in the frontier molecular-orbital energy gaps were apparently sensitive to the GQD size and edge shape. Small HOMO–LUMO gaps are known to be closely correlated with a large NLO response [3] because of the energetically favorable nature of electron addition to a high-lying LUMO orbital or electron extraction from a low-lying HOMO, as well as charge delocalization in the activated system [44]. Fig. 3 displays the second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal GQDs with different sizes. Results showed that size significantly influences the energy gap and second hyperpolarizability of GQDs. With the increase of carbon atom number in studied GQDs, from 18 to 96, the second hyperpolarizability increased by about 38 times, which may be due to the decrease in their frontier molecular-orbital energy gaps ( Fig. 2 ). A previous study [45] has shown that GQD has a finite-energy gap proportional to the inverse of the dot radius. Moreover, zigzag-edged GQDs had greater second hyperpolarizability than armchair-edged ones because the former had a smaller ΔEHL.

The second hyperpolarizabilities of zigzag-edged GQDs with vacancy and Stone–Wales defect were then calculated to determine the effects of structural defects on the third-order NLO response of GQDs. Compared with pristine GQDs (338.53 × 10−61 C4m4J−3 for GQD 2′) of the same size, the point-vacancy defect introduced through the removal of one carbon atom from pristine GQD resulted in an approximately 3% decrease in second hyperpolarizability (329.37 × 10−61 C4m4J−3). Conversely, the introduction of Stone–Wales defect led to an approximately 2% increase in second hyperpolarizability (345.18 × 10−61 C4m4J−3). Fig. 4 shows that the variation in frontier molecular-orbital energy gaps was sensitive to structural defects, which may also help elucidate the NLO activity of GQDs with defects. HOMO–LUMO gaps underwent significant changes in GQDs with defects. Compared with perfect GQDs, the gap decreased in the presence of Stone–Wales defect and increased with vacancy defect, consistent with the absorption spectroscopy results [46]. Small HOMO–LUMO gaps are known to be closely correlated with a high NLO response because in an excited state, charge transfer in the compound is energetically favorable [47].

The electronic structures and electronic second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal GQDs with structural defects were examined through the density functional theory. The effects of GQD size and edge effects on NLO properties were also studied. We found that with increased size, the second hyperpolarizability increased, resulting from the decrease in their frontier molecular-orbital energy gaps. The introduction of point vacancy limited the π-electron transport in the zigzag-edged GQDs and enlarged the frontier molecular-orbital energy gap. However, the energy gap decreased in the presence of Stone–Wales defect compared with perfect GQDs, which is benefited the enhancement in NLO response. Therefore, defective nanographene may be a new potential high-performance NLO material. Our findings provided valuable insights into graphene-based NLO materials for their further development.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China University of Mining and Technology (Grant No. 2019ZDPY16). H.P. Li would like to express the gratitude for the support by Prof. H.Q. Lin when he visited at CSRC. We are grateful to the Advanced Analysis and Computation Center of CUMT for the award of CPU hours to accomplish this work.
